lesson.title: Indexing & Selection
lesson.body: Pandas has two primary indexers: .loc[] uses label-based indexing (row/column names), while .iloc[] uses integer position-based indexing (0-based). Boolean selection filters rows where a condition is True: df[df['col'] > 5]. The .query() method offers SQL-like syntax. Use .at[] and .iat[] for scalar access. Chained indexing (df['a']['b']) can cause SettingWithCopyWarning — use .loc[] instead.

rooms.question: Complete the code to select rows 0-2 and columns 0-1 by position:
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'a':[1,2,3],'b':[4,5,6],'c':[7,8,9]})
result = df.___[0:3, 0:2]
rooms.explanation: df.iloc[row_positions, col_positions] — integer-based. df.iloc[0:3, 0:2] selects rows 0,1,2 and columns 0,1 (first two).

rooms.question: What does df.loc[2:5, 'name'] select?
answers: Rows with index labels 2 through 5 (inclusive), column 'name'
Rows at integer positions 2 to 4, column 'name'
rooms.explanation: .loc is label-based and INCLUSIVE of the stop label (unlike Python slicing). .iloc is position-based and exclusive of the stop.

rooms.question: Complete the boolean selection to filter rows where score > 80:
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'name':['A','B','C'],'score':[75,85,90]})
high = df[df['score'] ___ 80]
rooms.explanation: Boolean indexing: df[df['score'] > 80] creates a boolean mask and filters rows where it's True.

rooms.question: Complete the code to use .query() for the same filter:
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'name':['A','B','C'],'score':[75,85,90]})
high = df.___('score > 80')
rooms.explanation: df.query('condition') provides a clean SQL-like syntax. It's readable and efficient for complex conditions. Use @ for Python variables: df.query('score > @threshold').

rooms.question: Order these selection methods from label-based to position-based to scalar:
rooms.explanation: loc and at are label-based; iloc and iat are position-based. at/iat are scalar-optimized (much faster than loc/iloc for single values).

boss.name: The Index Phantom
boss.taunt: You tried to modify a copy, not the original. SettingWithCopyWarning awaits you.

questions.question: Why does df[df['a'] > 0]['b'] = 1 cause a SettingWithCopyWarning?
answers: The boolean selection may return a copy, so assignment may not modify the original df
Boolean indexing doesn't work with assignment
questions.explanation: Chained indexing may create a copy. Use df.loc[df['a'] > 0, 'b'] = 1 to guarantee in-place modification of the original DataFrame.

questions.question: Complete the correct way to set values with a condition:
questions.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'a':[1,-2,3],'b':[0,0,0]})
df.___[df['a'] > 0, 'b'] = 1
questions.explanation: df.loc[boolean_mask, column] = value is the correct pattern for conditional assignment. It directly modifies the original DataFrame in-place.

questions.question: df.iloc[-1] returns:
answers: The last row of the DataFrame
questions.explanation: Negative indexing works in .iloc just like Python lists: -1 is the last element, -2 is second-to-last, etc.
